introduction: Artificial Language problems invent a tiny coded language. Given translations of a few phrases, you deduce which code word maps to which English word, then translate a new phrase.
explanation:
CONCEPT

You receive several coded statements and their English meanings. By comparing overlapping phrases, you isolate the code for each word (or root). Then you build or decode a new sentence.

HOW TO SOLVE

1) Align phrases that share an English word and find the shared code token.
2) Eliminate codes already assigned.
3) Watch for plurals, tense, or adjective endings if the puzzle uses them.
4) Order in the code may or may not match English order—follow evidence from examples.
5) Translate the target using only confirmed mappings.

IMPORTANT RULES

• Shared meaning ⇒ shared code element.
• Unique meaning in one phrase helps assign leftover codes.
• Do not assume English word order unless examples prove it.
• One code word usually maps to one English word (unless stated otherwise).

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Matching by position alone without overlap checks.
2) Assigning two English words to one code.
3) Ignoring a word that appears in only one sentence until the end.
4) Changing a confirmed mapping to fit a wrong option.
5) Forgetting articles (a/the) if they are coded separately.

formulas.title: Overlap rule
formulas.content: If phrase1 and phrase2 share English word W, their shared code token is likely W

formulas.title: Elimination
formulas.content: After assigning known codes, remaining code ↔ remaining word

formulas.title: Consistency check
formulas.content: Every occurrence of a code must match the same English word

formulas.title: Build translation
formulas.content: Replace each English word by its code (or reverse) using the map

shortcutTips.title: Start with repeats
shortcutTips.tip: Words that appear in two phrases are the easiest to decode first.

shortcutTips.title: Make a table
shortcutTips.tip: Write English word → code in a small list as you confirm each pair.

shortcutTips.title: Use leftovers
shortcutTips.tip: When only one word and one code remain in a phrase, they match.

shortcutTips.title: Order last
shortcutTips.tip: Decide word order only after meanings are fixed.

solvedExamples.problem: If "pit na sa" means "you are welcome", "na dig pit" means "you are good", and "sa ti" means "welcome friend", what is the code for "friend"?
steps: "you are" appears in first two → shared codes pit, na.
Third phrase "sa ti" = welcome friend → ti = friend.

solvedExamples.problem: If "la mi" means "red apple" and "mi to" means "sweet apple", code for red?
steps: Shared word apple → shared code mi.
Therefore la = red and to = sweet.

solvedExamples.problem: Given map: sun=kor, bright=vel, day=nip. Encode "bright sun".
solvedExamples.solution: vel kor (order as English unless examples say otherwise).

references.title: Substitution ciphers and coded language logic
